title: The Lord on the Shore
subtitle: The risen Christ appears to seven disciples at the Sea of Tiberias

stillness: Be still, and come to the shore of the sea.
Let the long, fruitless night fall silent within you.
Wait here, in the morning light, for One who stands and is not yet known.
gospel: After an interval of time, Jesus showed himself again to the disciples at the Sea of Tiberias.
Seven were fishing together one night, that they might have food.
Simon Peter, and Thomas called Didymus, and Nathanael of Cana in Galilee, and the sons of Zebedee, and two others of his disciples.
Through that whole night they labored in vain, and took nothing.
But when morning came, Jesus stood on the shore.
Yet the disciples did not know that it was Jesus.
Jesus said to them: Children, have you any food to eat?
He said to them: Cast the net on the right side of the boat, and you will find.
They cast it, and now they were not able to draw it, for the multitude of fishes.
Then that disciple whom Jesus loved said to Peter: It is the Lord.
When Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he girded his tunic about him, for he was naked,
and he cast himself into the sea, that he might come to him the sooner.
behold: Attend here to the things that were done, for they are most full of joy.
He did not appear upon the water, but on the solid land,
to show that he was no longer with them in the waves of this corruptible life,
but had passed over to the state of immortal life.
He does not at once show himself, that through the miracle he was about to work they might know him.
He speaks for a while in a more human way, as one wishing to buy fish from them.
See John, the virgin, purer than the rest and keener in knowing:
considering the miracle accomplished as it had been foretold, he recognizes him first.
And see Peter: naked, that is, but lightly clothed, the readier for the work of fishing.
He could not wait for the slow boat,
but, driven by the immensity of love, he hurled himself into the sea,
that he might come more swiftly to his Lord.
consider: Through the whole night they labored in vain and took nothing, because the help of God was absent — and the lack of it may rightly be called night.
So too the preacher's tongue labors in vain without Christ's inward instruction; without his power they catch nothing, and can do nothing.
But when morning comes, when the light comes that lifts up hearts, Christ himself chiefly working, they catch a great multitude of fishes.
The net of Christ is the word of God; the fishes are all sinners; the right side, the things of the spirit; the left, the things of time.
He who preaches for worldly gain or empty glory fishes on the left, and his nets are foul, and he profits little.
But he who in his teaching seeks the salvation of his hearers fishes on the right, and his nets are fragrant, and he profits much.
John was keener in mind, Peter more fervent in heart.
Therefore John was the first to know Jesus, but Peter was the first to go to him.
Purity of mind and body most disposes a man to the knowledge of divine things.

By the contemplative life, Peter, you are told: if in the day of action you have been busy, at least in the night let the contemplative life be lawful to you — be not so torn apart by works that you never look upon him.
Everywhere Peter is found of more fervent faith and greater love.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me, a wretched man, to set before you the food you ask,
for you are in a manner refreshed by our obedience, and in it you take the greatest delight.
Make me also, unworthy, a sharer of your banquet,
that I may be refreshed by you, the fish roasted and suffered for us, by suffering for you,
and be filled by you, the true bread, now in your teaching and the sacrament of your Body,
and at last in the joy of the eternal fellowship.
Grant me also, Lord, that with Peter I may be worthy to love you, bearing evils for your sake,
and with John to be loved by you, clinging to you;
and ever to grow in this, and to persevere unto the end. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Petrus quidem moras navis exspectare non potuit, sed amoris immensitate cogente, in mare se praecipitavit, ut velocius et magis festinanter ad Dominum suum venire posset.
latinJewel.english: Peter could not wait for the slowness of the boat, but, driven by the immensity of love, he hurled himself into the sea, that he might come more swiftly and in greater haste to his Lord.
